costs: Statutory transport service capital and recurring operating costs (vehicles, drivers, fuel, supervision), not yet validated against Hungarian route-specific data
Capital investment for expanded consolidated 'centros escolares'-style receiving-site capacity
TEIP-style targeted support staffing and programme costs at consolidated sites, recurring annually
Indirect community-level costs (property-value decline, reduced local employer base) documented in comparative literature but not typically included in official budget accounting
content.equity_impact: Disproportionately burdens lower-SES and Roma families concentrated in peripheral rural settlements, who face longer travel distances, higher risk of dropout (Roma dropout already 58.7% vs. 9.3% non-Roma), and the documented risk that non-Roma/wealthier families exit consolidated hub schools via free school choice ('white flight'), reproducing segregation at the hub-school scale; without specific, enforceable thresholds (e.g., maximum travel time such as Portugal's Fundão 25-minute rule), disaggregated equity-outcome monitoring by ethnicity and income at the consolidated site, and binding (not merely advisory) transport-safety standards, the compensatory measures in this scenario risk remaining declarative rather than protective.
expected_benefits: Enables investment concentration in fewer, better-equipped consolidated sites, following the Portuguese centros escolares pattern of matched receiving-site investment.
Statutory transport guarantees and targeted support provide a formal, legally enforceable floor of protection for displaced pupils, an improvement over ad hoc arrangements.

May reduce per-pupil operating costs at the system level by concentrating specialist staff and facilities, though empirical audits of actual realized savings (net of transport and mitigation costs) are scarce.
content.goal: Close low-enrollment village schools and reassign pupils to a consolidated center school, paired with statutory transport guarantees, targeted support (TEIP-style), and transparent cost/consultation rules to offset travel and equity harms.

implementation_steps.action: Conduct and publish transparent cost/consultation disclosure prior to closure decision, including independent expert assessment where legally required, respecting the statutory May-deadline procedural window.
implementation_steps.timeline: year 1, ahead of each May decision deadline

implementation_steps.action: Establish statutory transport guarantees with route-specific safety and capacity audits before pupil reassignment, addressing the unaccompanied-minor gap documented in current informal coping mechanisms.
Deploy TEIP-style targeted support staffing with disaggregated outcome monitoring by ethnicity and income at the consolidated site.

mechanisms: Portugal's 2005/2006 school network reorganization paired the closure of 1,418 schools with construction of 800 larger, better-equipped 'centros escolares,' demonstrating that closure can be paired with matched receiving-site investment.

Post-closure transportation costs have been cited in Central European contexts in a range of roughly 800-1,500 EUR per pupil per year, though this precise figure has not been validated against Hungarian route lengths, occupancy rates, or regional terrain, and often offsets part of the projected consolidation savings.

Portugal's TEIP compensatory programme reduced dropout and failure rates in disadvantaged school clusters but failed over 15 years to close the performance gap between TEIP and non-TEIP schools on national exams, indicating access/retention support alone does not guarantee learning-outcome equity.

An Italian study exploiting a school-network contraction reform found that municipalities affected by school closures experienced significant population and income declines beyond pre-existing demographic trends, indicating a causal feedback loop that mitigation measures would need to specifically counteract.
risks: Compensatory measures (transport guarantees, TEIP-style support) require sustained multi-year funding; without a specified binding funding-commitment mechanism (e.g., ring-fenced budget line, multi-year statutory allocation), these measures risk being cut in future budget cycles once the initial closure has already occurred and political attention has moved on.

Closure decisions, even with consultation, may trigger community mobilization (as in the 2007 Erdőkürt 'Human Chain for Rural Schools' precedent), and limited judicial review (legality-only, not policy-merit) means affected communities have few formal channels to contest closure substance.
content.title: Consolidation with mitigation (closure as default, compensatory measures)
